concepts.name: naive bayes classifier
concepts.description: A classification method that assumes features are independent given the class, dramatically simplifying density estimation by estimating only univariate marginal densities. Despite strong assumptions, it often outperforms more sophisticated alternatives.
concepts.existing_quote: The naive Bayes model assumes that given a class G = j, the features Xk are independent. Despite these rather optimistic assumptions, naive Bayes classifiers often outperform far more sophisticated alternatives.

concepts.name: renormalized radial basis functions
concepts.description: A modification of radial basis functions that normalizes each basis function by the sum of all basis functions, avoiding holes (regions where no kernel has appreciable support) when using constant scale parameters.
concepts.existing_quote: Renormalized radial basis functions hⱼ(x) = D(||x-ξⱼ||/λ) / Σₖ D(||x-ξₖ||/λ) avoid this problem.

concepts.name: gaussian mixture model
concepts.description: A density estimation method representing a distribution as a weighted sum of Gaussian densities with mixing proportions αₘ, means μₘ, and covariance matrices Σₘ, typically fit by maximum likelihood using the EM algorithm.
concepts.existing_quote: The Gaussian mixture model has the form f(x) = Σₘ αₘ φ(x; μₘ, Σₘ) with mixing proportions αₘ, Σₘ αₘ = 1, and each Gaussian density has a mean μₘ and covariance matrix Σₘ.

concepts.name: model selection
concepts.description: The task of estimating the performance of different models in order to choose the best one, typically by estimating prediction error for various tuning parameters.
concepts.existing_quote: Model selection: estimating the performance of different models in order to choose the best one.

concepts.name: model assessment
concepts.description: The task of estimating the prediction error (generalization error) of a final chosen model on new data, ideally using a held-out test set kept in a vault.
concepts.existing_quote: Model assessment: having chosen a final model, estimating its prediction error (generalization error) on new data.

concepts.name: model bias
concepts.description: The error between the best-fitting model in a restricted class and the true function, representing limitations of the model family itself rather than estimation error.
concepts.existing_quote: The first term on the right-hand side is the average squared model bias, the error between the best-fitting linear approximation and the true function.

concepts.name: optimism
concepts.description: The difference between in-sample error and training error, representing how much training error underestimates true error. For many loss functions, optimism = (2/N)Σᵢ Cov(ŷᵢ, yᵢ).
concepts.existing_quote: We define the optimism as the difference between Err_in and the training error err. The amount by which err underestimates the true error depends on how strongly yᵢ affects its own prediction.

concepts.name: effective number of parameters
concepts.description: For linear fitting methods ŷ = Sy, defined as trace(S), generalizing the concept of number of parameters to regularized models. Replaces d in the Cₚ statistic for non-standard fits.
concepts.existing_quote: The effective number of parameters is defined as df(S) = trace(S), the sum of the diagonal elements of S (also known as the effective degrees-of-freedom).

concepts.name: vapnik-chervonenkis dimension
concepts.description: A measure of model complexity defined as the largest number of points (in some configuration) that can be shattered by members of a function class. A set of points is shattered if the class can perfectly separate any binary labeling of them.
concepts.existing_quote: The VC dimension of the class {f(x,α)} is defined to be the largest number of points (in some configuration) that can be shattered by members of {f(x,α)}. A set of points is said to be shattered by a class of functions if, no matter how we assign a binary label to each point, a member of the class can perfectly separate them.

concepts.name: structural risk minimization
concepts.description: Vapnik's approach to model selection that fits a nested sequence of models of increasing VC dimensions and chooses the model minimizing an upper bound on test error based on VC theory.
concepts.existing_quote: Vapnik's structural risk minimization (SRM) approach fits a nested sequence of models of increasing VC dimensions h₁ < h₂ < ··· , and then chooses the model with the smallest value of the upper bound.

concepts.name: k-fold cross-validation
concepts.description: A method for estimating prediction error by splitting data into K parts, fitting the model to K-1 parts and testing on the remaining part, then averaging the K prediction errors. Typical choices are K=5 or K=10.
concepts.existing_quote: We split the data into K roughly equal-sized parts; for the kth part, we fit the model to the other K−1 parts of the data, and calculate the prediction error of the fitted model when predicting the kth part of the data. We do this for k=1,2,...,K and combine the K estimates of prediction error.

concepts.name: one-standard-error rule
concepts.description: A model selection heuristic used with cross-validation that chooses the most parsimonious model whose error is no more than one standard error above the error of the best model.
concepts.existing_quote: Often a 'one-standard error' rule is used with cross-validation, in which we choose the most parsimonious model whose error is no more than one standard error above the error of the best model.

concepts.name: tri-cube kernel
concepts.description: A popular compact support kernel function that is flatter on top than the Epanechnikov kernel and is differentiable at the boundary of its support, used in local regression.
concepts.existing_quote: Another popular compact kernel is based on the tri-cube function. This is flatter on the top (like the nearest-neighbor box) and is differentiable at the boundary of its support.

concepts.name: boundary bias in kernel methods
concepts.description: A bias problem in kernel smoothing where locally-weighted averages are badly biased on the boundaries of the domain because of kernel asymmetry. Local linear regression corrects this bias exactly to first order.
concepts.existing_quote: Locally-weighted averages can be badly biased on the boundaries of the domain, because of the asymmetry of the kernel in that region. By fitting straight lines rather than constants locally, we can remove this bias exactly to first order

concepts.name: curse of dimensionality in local regression
concepts.description: The phenomenon that in high dimensions, it becomes impossible to simultaneously maintain localness (low bias) and a sizable neighborhood sample (low variance) without exponential sample size growth. The fraction of boundary points approaches one as dimension grows.
concepts.existing_quote: One of the manifestations of the curse of dimensionality is that the fraction of points close to the boundary increases to one as the dimension grows. It is impossible to simultaneously maintain localness (⇒low bias) and a sizable sample in the neighborhood (⇒low variance) as the dimension increases, without the total sample size increasing exponentially in p.

concepts.name: anova decomposition in regression
concepts.description: A decomposition of the regression function into main effects, two-way interactions, and higher-order interactions: f(X) = α + Σⱼ gⱼ(Xⱼ) + Σⱼ<ₖ gⱼₖ(Xⱼ,Xₖ) + ···, used to introduce structure by eliminating higher-order terms.
concepts.existing_quote: It is natural to consider analysis-of-variance (ANOVA) decompositions of the form f(X) = α + Σⱼ gⱼ(Xⱼ) + Σⱼ<ₖ gⱼₖ(Xⱼ,Xₖ) + ··· and then introduce structure by eliminating some of the higher-order terms.

concepts.name: structured kernels
concepts.description: Kernels using a positive semidefinite matrix A to weight different coordinates: Kλ,A(x₀,x) = D(||(x-x₀)ᵀA||/λ), allowing individual predictors to be downgraded or entire directions to be omitted.
concepts.existing_quote: A more general approach is to use a positive semidefinite matrix A to weigh the different coordinates: Kλ,A(x₀,x) = D(||(x-x₀)ᵀA||/λ). Entire coordinates or directions can be downgraded or omitted by imposing appropriate restrictions on A.

concepts.name: instantaneous prefix code
concepts.description: A coding scheme where no code is the prefix of any other, allowing the receiver to know exactly when a message has been completely sent without requiring delimiters.
concepts.existing_quote: This code is known as an instantaneous prefix code: no code is the prefix of any other, and the receiver (who knows all of the possible codes), knows exactly when the message has been completely sent.

concepts.name: learning curve
concepts.description: A plot of prediction performance (e.g., 1-Err) versus training set size N, showing how classifier performance improves with more data and used to assess cross-validation bias.
concepts.existing_quote: Figure 7.8 shows a hypothetical 'learning curve' for a classifier on a given task, a plot of 1−Err versus the size of the training set N. The performance of the classifier improves as the training set size increases

concepts.name: local linear regression
concepts.description: A locally weighted regression technique that fits straight lines rather than constants locally by solving weighted least squares problems at each target point, which removes boundary bias exactly to first order.
concepts.existing_quote: Locally weighted regression solves a separate weighted least squares problem at each target point x0

paragraphs.text:
• This leaves a more general problem to deal with: observation weights
wi. Operationally we simply multiply them by the kernel weights be-
fore computing the weighted average. With nearest neighborhoods, it
is now natural to insist on neighborhoods with a total weight content
k (relative to 	 wi). In the event of overﬂow (the last observation
needed in a neighborhood has a weight wj which causes the sum of
weights to exceed the budget k), then fractional parts can be used.

• Boundary issues arise. The metric neighborhoods tend to contain less
points on the boundaries, while the nearest-neighborhoods get wider.

• The Epanechnikov kernel has compact support (needed when used
with nearest-neighbor window size). Another popular compact kernel
is based on the tri-cube function

This is ﬂatter on the top (like the nearest-neighbor box) and is diﬀer-
entiable at the boundary of its support. The Gaussian density func-
tion D(t) = φ(t) is a popular noncompact kernel, with the standard-
deviation playing the role of the window size. Figure 6.2 compares
the three.

We have progressed from the raw moving average to a smoothly varying
locally weighted average by using kernel weighting. The smooth kernel ﬁt
still has problems, however, as exhibited in Figure 6.3 (left panel). Locally-
weighted averages can be badly biased on the boundaries of the domain,
